title: Today's AI Brief: Vatican Meets Silicon Valley
summary: The Vatican's unprecedented alliance with Anthropic, rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI, and the chaos caused by AI agents in the tech world
imageAlt: Image from source article for Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageCaption: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation
imageSourceTitle: Why the Vatican Invited Anthropic to the Pope’s AI Encyclical Presentation

body.text: I'm looking at a fascinating story about the Vatican inviting Anthropic, a Silicon Valley company, to the Pope's AI encyclical presentation. This move signals an unprecedented alliance between the Catholic church and the tech world. But what's behind this partnership? According to Wired, the Vatican wanted to explore the potential of AI to improve people's lives, and Anthropic's cofounder, Christopher Olah, was the perfect person to speak about it. It's interesting to see how the Vatican is taking a proactive approach to understanding AI and its potential impact on society.

The news item that caught my attention is about the Vatican's invitation to Anthropic. It's interesting to see how the Catholic church is embracing AI and its potential to drive positive change. Another story that stands out is about rethinking organizational design in the age of agentic AI. As reported by Technology Review, many organizations want to adopt agentic AI, but their current infrastructure can't support it. It's like trying to put a square peg in a round hole – it just won't work. This highlights the need for companies to reassess their organizational design and infrastructure to accommodate the growing demands of agentic AI.

I also found it intriguing that 85% of organizations want to be agentic within the next three years, but 76% say their current operations and infrastructure can't support that change. This discrepancy shows that there's a significant gap between ambition and execution when it comes to adopting agentic AI. It will be interesting to see how companies bridge this gap and adapt to the changing landscape of AI.

I also came across a story about AI agents causing chaos in the tech world. It's like a scene from a sci-fi movie, where AI agents are taking over and causing havoc. According to Wired, some techies have even formed a support group, called Claude Code Anonymous, to deal with their addiction to coding tools like Anthropic's Claude. It's amusing to see how AI has become such a big part of our lives that we need support groups to cope with it. On a more serious note, the chaos caused by AI agents highlights the need for better regulation and oversight in the tech industry.

The story of Peter Steinberger, who addressed a meetup in London called Claude Code Anonymous, is particularly interesting. He and some fellow addicts had arranged the event to network with people like themselves – techies swept up by coding tools such as Anthropic's paradigm-busting AI. It's a unique perspective on the human side of the AI revolution and how it's affecting people's lives.

items: The Vatican's alliance with Anthropic signals a new era of collaboration between the tech world and religious institutions
Rethinking organizational design is crucial for companies that want to adopt agentic AI
AI agents are causing chaos in the tech world, but also driving innovation and growth
The human side of the AI revolution is just as important as the technological advancements

body.text: As I wrap up this AI brief, I'm reminded of how fast the tech world is moving. It's like trying to drink from a firehose – there's just so much information coming at us from all directions. But what stands out to me is the potential of AI to drive positive change, whether it's through the Vatican's alliance with Anthropic or the adoption of agentic AI in organizations. It's an exciting time to be alive, and I'm looking forward to seeing how AI continues to shape our world. For me, the most interesting aspect of AI is its ability to bring people together, whether it's through a support group for coding addicts or a collaboration between the tech world and religious institutions.
